title.emphasis: Part I: The Architecture of Control – Acceptable Use Policies as a Private Regulatory Regime

section.para: The modern digital economy is built upon a critical, yet largely invisible, infrastructure: the financial networks that process payments. While often perceived as neutral conduits for commerce, a small number of dominant payment intermediaries have evolved into powerful private regulators. Through the imposition of vaguely worded and unilaterally enforced Acceptable Use Policies (AUPs), these financial gatekeepers function as an extra-legal body, dictating the boundaries of acceptable speech and commerce online. This regime is characterized by its lack of transparency, accountability, and due process, creating a system where lawful expression can be suppressed at the sole discretion of a corporate entity.

title.emphasis: 1.1 The Duopoly of Control: Card Networks and Payment Processors

section.para: The power to regulate online commerce is highly centralized. A functional duopoly of card networks, namely Visa and Mastercard, establishes the overarching rules and standards for the entire ecosystem.1 Payment processors, such as PayPal and Stripe, are contractually bound to adhere to and enforce these network standards as a condition of accessing their payment rails.4 This hierarchical structure creates a choke point through which a vast majority of online transactions must pass.

For digital platforms that serve as marketplaces for creators—such as the gaming platforms Steam and Itch.io or creator-funding sites like Patreon—access to these payment networks is not a luxury but an existential necessity.6 The threat of being cut off from Visa or Mastercard, and by extension from processors like Stripe and PayPal, gives these financial intermediaries immense leverage. Consequently, when pressure is applied by the card networks, often at the behest of activist groups, platforms are compelled to comply by de-platforming entire categories of legal content to protect their core payment infrastructure.6 This dynamic demonstrates that the locus of control over online commerce often lies not with the platforms themselves, but with the financial gatekeepers they depend on.

title.emphasis: 1.2 The Language of Discretion: Analyzing Vague and Overbroad AUPs

section.para: The primary instrument of this control is the Acceptable Use Policy, a contractual document characterized by its use of vague, subjective, and overbroad language. A review of the AUPs for major processors reveals a pattern of prohibitions that are not tied to clear legal standards but to the processor's own discretionary judgment. These policies are classic contracts of adhesion, presented on a "take-it-or-leave-it" basis where the merchant has no power to negotiate terms.10

This discretionary power is codified in the language of the policies themselves. For example, processors frequently ban transactions related to "obscene" or "certain sexually oriented materials," "hate or harmful products," or any activity that, in the processor's opinion, might cause "reputational damage" or is "brand-damaging".3 The recent, and highly controversial, addition of "misinformation" to PayPal's list of prohibited activities further illustrates the expansion of this discretionary power into the realm of protected speech.15 The enforceability of these policies is anchored in clauses, often found within the broader User or Services Agreements, that grant the processor "sole discretion" to interpret and apply its own rules, effectively making it the judge, jury, and executioner of its private regulatory regime.16
The following table provides a comparative analysis of the vague and discretionary language found in the AUPs of major payment processors and card networks.

row.entry: "the promotion of hate, violence, racial or other forms of intolerance that is discriminatory" 3
Prohibits activities that cause or threaten "reputational damage to us or any Card Network" 13
Determined by Stripe's "review upon account activation" 5
"[p]ornography and other mature audience content (including literature, imagery and other media) depicting nudity or explicit sexual acts" 3
Reserves the right to ban merchants for "brand-damaging Transaction activity" 3

title.emphasis: 1.3 The Absence of Due Process: Unilateral Enforcement and the Lack of Recourse

section.para: The enforcement of these vague policies is as opaque as their language. Creators and businesses frequently report having their accounts suspended or terminated, and their funds frozen, with little to no specific explanation beyond a generic reference to an AUP violation.19 Reports from civil liberties organizations like the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF) document a systemic failure by processors to provide users with detailed notice of the alleged violation or a meaningful and timely opportunity to appeal the decision.15 This lack of due process transforms the AUP from a contractual term into an instrument of arbitrary power, leaving creators with no effective recourse to challenge what may be a mistaken or biased enforcement action.

This system functions as a form of privatized censorship. While government entities are bound by constitutional principles like the First Amendment when regulating speech, private payment processors are not state actors and operate outside these constraints.24 By establishing and enforcing a global speech code based on their own corporate values, they effectively circumvent democratic processes and legal precedent, creating what one commentator described as a "security hole that endangers democracy itself".26

The intentional vagueness of the AUPs, combined with the opaque and punitive nature of their enforcement, creates a powerful chilling effect on legal expression. When creators cannot reliably predict what content will be deemed a violation, the only rational course of action is to self-censor, avoiding any topic that could be perceived as controversial.8 This induced self-censorship is a far more efficient and scalable method of content moderation than direct enforcement, as it pre-emptively purges the marketplace of disfavored but legal speech, profoundly shaping the digital public sphere.7
